title: 07 — Evaluate and migrate to TypeScript 7 when the toolchain is compatible
objective: Evaluate the TypeScript 7 major upgrade and migrate the repository only when the complete application, linting, build, deployment, and automation toolchain supports it without weakening validation or relying on unsupported workarounds.
userContext: Dependabot proposes TypeScript 7.0.2 while the repository currently uses TypeScript 5.9.3. Maintainers need major compiler upgrades to be assessed as coordinated compatibility changes across Next.js, ESLint, typescript-eslint, OpenNext, Wrangler, Cloudflare Worker builds, generated type configuration, repository scripts, and editor tooling rather than merged as routine dependency updates.
currentBehaviour: The repository uses the stable TypeScript 5.9 toolchain established by DEV-004. Dependabot can propose TypeScript 7, but the repository does not yet have a documented compatibility decision or migration gate covering all tools that invoke or embed TypeScript.
requiredBehaviour: The repository must assess TypeScript 7 compatibility across the complete supported toolchain and either migrate to the latest stable mutually compatible TypeScript 7 release or explicitly defer the migration with documented blockers and reassessment conditions.
userExperience: Developers continue using the existing lint, typecheck, test, build, and deployment commands with strict validation. Maintainers can see whether TypeScript 7 is supported, what evidence was reviewed, and why the migration proceeded or was deferred.
functionalRequirements: Review official TypeScript 7 release notes, migration documentation, compiler changes, configuration changes, and compatibility guidance.
Record the material behavioural and architectural differences between the current TypeScript 5.9 toolchain and TypeScript 7.

Verify TypeScript 7 compatibility with Next.js, eslint-config-next, ESLint, typescript-eslint, React type definitions, Node.js 24 type definitions, OpenNext Cloudflare, Wrangler, Cloudflare Worker builds, repository scripts, tests, and editor tooling.
Inspect documented support ranges and package peer dependencies rather than relying only on successful npm dependency resolution.
Identify packages and repository tooling that invoke or embed the TypeScript compiler or compiler API.
Determine whether Next.js generated types, incremental compilation, editor integration, type generation, or compiler configuration behave differently under TypeScript 7.
Permit a migration outcome only when the latest stable TypeScript 7 release is mutually compatible with the required repository toolchain.
Permit a deferral outcome when a critical dependency or integration does not yet support TypeScript 7.
When migration proceeds, apply required official migrations and resolve new compiler, source, configuration, and generated-type issues properly.
Do not use broad suppressions, unsafe casts, blanket exclusions, unsupported package overrides, weakened compiler settings, or disabled validation merely to make the migration pass.
Preserve strict compiler settings and existing linting, type checking, testing, build, deployment, and workflow-security coverage.
Regenerate package-lock.json using the canonical package-lock repair workflow rather than manually editing generated lockfile metadata.
Review changes generated in next-env.d.ts, tsconfig.json, .next/types, and other framework-generated TypeScript configuration.
Commit generated configuration changes only when they are required, understood, stable, and appropriate for source control.
When migration is deferred, retain the latest supported TypeScript version and document the blocking dependency, evidence reviewed, and condition that should trigger reassessment.
When migration is deferred, ensure the unsupported Dependabot major update is closed, ignored, or otherwise prevented from being treated as an automatically mergeable routine update.
Update repository documentation and specs/000-current-application-spec.md with the selected compatibility decision and migration or deferral outcome.
technicalConsiderations: This feature follows DEV-004 and should normally be implemented after DEV-005 and DEV-006 so the assessment reflects the maintained dependency and automation model.
Treat TypeScript 7 as a coordinated major toolchain migration rather than a routine direct-dependency refresh.
Successful compilation or npm installation alone does not prove compatibility with tools that use the TypeScript compiler programmatically.
Validate both the TypeScript CLI and every directly relevant integration using TypeScript APIs or generated configuration.
Do not weaken strict mode, lint rules, compiler options, tests, workflow-security controls, application builds, Worker builds, or deployment validation.
Do not use prerelease, nightly, experimental, or otherwise unsupported production packages to force compatibility.
Directly related linting, framework, or type-definition packages may be updated only where required for TypeScript 7 compatibility.
Do not manually edit generated package-lock.json internals.
Use the canonical Node.js version from .nvmrc and npm version from package.json#packageManager.
The implementation must explicitly produce either a supported migration decision or a documented deferral decision.
outOfScope: Implementing TypeScript 7 as part of the feature-request JSON pull request.
Framework major-version upgrades unless strictly required for supported TypeScript 7 compatibility.
Bypassing peer-dependency or documented compatibility constraints.
Using unsupported package overrides or forced dependency resolution.
Weakening TypeScript, ESLint, testing, build, deployment, or workflow-security validation.
Treating the Dependabot recommendation as proof that the complete repository toolchain supports TypeScript 7.
acceptanceCriteria: TypeScript 7 compatibility has been assessed against every directly relevant application, compiler, linting, type-definition, build, deployment, automation, and editor integration.
The compatibility evidence and the decision to migrate or defer are documented.
If migration proceeds, the repository uses the latest stable mutually compatible TypeScript 7 release.
If migration is deferred, the repository remains on the latest supported TypeScript release and clearly documents the blocking dependency or integration and reassessment condition.
No compiler, linting, type checking, testing, build, deployment, or workflow-security validation is weakened.
New type and configuration errors are resolved properly rather than hidden through suppressions, unsafe casts, broad exclusions, or weakened options.
A clean npm ci succeeds with the canonical Node.js and npm toolchain.
npm run toolchain:validate, npm test, npm run lint, npm run typecheck, npm run build, npm run build:worker, and npm run issue:validate pass when migration proceeds.
Repository workflow and security tests pass.
Cloudflare preview or deployment validation passes where credentials and external services permit.
A second clean dependency installation produces no unexplained package-lock.json drift.
Framework-generated TypeScript files leave no unexplained tracked changes.
The canonical package-lock repair workflow produces a stable single-file lockfile update where required.
Dependabot handling reflects the migration or deferral decision.
Documentation and specs/000-current-application-spec.md reflect the outcome.
codexGuidance: Implement after DEV-006. Begin with a documented compatibility assessment and do not assume TypeScript 7 is safe merely because Dependabot proposes it or npm resolves it. If a critical compatibility gate fails, produce a focused documented deferral instead of forcing the migration.
